reading.title: Text 1: A New Life in a New City
reading.content:
Sarah moved to Edinburgh six months ago. Before that, she lived in a small village in the south of England with her parents and her younger sister, Emma. Sarah always dreamed of living in a big city, so when she got a job offer from a marketing company in Edinburgh, she was very excited.

At first, life in Edinburgh was not easy. Sarah didn't know anyone in the city, and she felt lonely. Her flat was small and cold, and the weather was much colder and rainier than in the south of England. She missed her family and her old friends. She called her mother almost every day and sometimes she cried after hanging up the phone.

However, things started to get better after a few weeks. Sarah joined a yoga class at a gym near her flat, and she met a woman called Fiona. Fiona was from Edinburgh and she knew the city very well. She showed Sarah all the best places to eat, shop and have fun. They quickly became good friends. Fiona introduced Sarah to her other friends, and soon Sarah had a small group of people to spend time with at the weekends.

Sarah also started to enjoy her job. Her colleagues were friendly and helpful, and her manager, Mr Robertson, was patient and kind. He gave her interesting projects to work on, and Sarah learned a lot about marketing in her first few months. She worked hard and her manager noticed. After four months, he told her that she was doing an excellent job and that the company was very happy with her work.

Now, six months later, Sarah loves her life in Edinburgh. She has a comfortable flat in a nice area of the city, she has good friends, and she enjoys her job. She still misses her family, but she visits them every month. Her sister Emma is planning to move to Edinburgh too, and Sarah is looking forward to that very much.

Last weekend, Sarah went for a walk with Fiona in the old part of the city. They visited Edinburgh Castle, walked along the Royal Mile and stopped at a small café for a cup of tea and a slice of cake. It was a cold but sunny day, and the views from the castle were amazing. Sarah took a lot of photos and sent them to her mother. Her mother replied, "It looks beautiful. I can see why you love it there."

Sarah smiled and thought, "Yes, this is my home now."

1. Where did Sarah live before she moved to Edinburgh?
2. Why was life in Edinburgh difficult at first for Sarah?
3. How did Sarah meet her friend Fiona?
4. What did Sarah's manager tell her after four months?

reading.title: Text 2: The Best Holiday of My Life
reading.content:
My name is Pedro and I want to tell you about the best holiday of my life. Last summer, my wife Ana and I travelled to the south of Italy for two weeks. We visited three cities: Rome, Naples and a small town on the Amalfi Coast called Positano. It was our first time in Italy, and we loved every moment of it.

We flew from São Paulo to Rome. The flight was very long — about twelve hours — but we were so excited that we didn't mind. When we arrived at Rome airport, we took a taxi to our hotel. The hotel was in the centre of the city, near the Colosseum. We could see the Colosseum from our bedroom window! We couldn't believe it.

On our first day in Rome, we walked around the city and visited the main tourist attractions. We saw the Colosseum, the Roman Forum and the Trevi Fountain. Ana threw a coin into the fountain because, according to the tradition, that means you will return to Rome one day. We also visited the Vatican City and saw the Sistine Chapel. The paintings on the ceiling were absolutely incredible. I took hundreds of photos.

The food in Rome was amazing. We ate pasta almost every day, and it was the best pasta I have ever eaten in my life. We also tried pizza, of course, and gelato — Italian ice cream. My favourite flavour was pistachio. Ana preferred strawberry. Every evening, we sat at a small restaurant near our hotel, drank red wine and watched the people walking by. It was so relaxing.

After five days in Rome, we took the train to Naples. Naples is a very different city — it is noisier, more chaotic and less touristy than Rome, but it has a lot of character. The people there were very warm and friendly. We tried the famous Neapolitan pizza, which is the original Italian pizza, and it was absolutely delicious. We also visited the National Archaeological Museum, which has many artefacts from Pompeii.

The last part of our trip was the best. We took a bus from Naples to Positano, a beautiful town on the Amalfi Coast. The bus journey along the coast was incredible — the road goes along the edge of the cliffs, high above the sea. The views were breathtaking. I was a little scared because the road was very narrow and the bus was very close to the edge, but Ana just laughed and took photos.

Positano is a small town built on a steep hill. The houses are painted in different colours — yellow, pink, white and orange — and they go down the hill to the beach. We stayed in a small guest house with a balcony that had a view of the sea. Every morning, we had breakfast on the balcony — fresh bread, cheese, fruit and strong Italian coffee. Then we went down to the beach and spent the day swimming in the clear blue water and reading books under an umbrella.

When it was time to go home, we were very sad. Ana cried a little at the airport. We both agreed that it was the best holiday we had ever had. We are already saving money to go back next year. This time, we want to visit Florence, Venice and the Italian lakes. I can't wait!

**Glossary / Glossário:**

| we didn't mind | não nos importamos |

**Comprehension Questions / Perguntas de Interpretação:**

1. How long was Pedro and Ana's holiday in Italy?
2. Why did Ana throw a coin into the Trevi Fountain?
3. How does Pedro describe Naples compared to Rome?
4. What scared Pedro during the bus journey to Positano?
5. What are Pedro and Ana planning for their next holiday?

reading.title: Text 3: Working from Home
reading.content:
The way people work has changed a lot in recent years. Before 2020, most office workers went to an office every day. They woke up early, got dressed in smart clothes, drove or took public transport to work, and spent eight hours or more sitting at a desk in an office building. At the end of the day, they travelled home again, often in heavy traffic. Many people spent one or two hours a day just commuting.

Then, in 2020, something changed. Because of the global pandemic, millions of people around the world had to stay at home. Companies quickly discovered that many jobs could be done from home. Workers set up offices in their bedrooms, living rooms and kitchens. They used their laptops and the internet to do their work, and they used video calls to talk to their colleagues and attend meetings. At first, many people found it strange and difficult, but over time, a lot of workers started to enjoy working from home.

There are many advantages of working from home. First, workers save time because they don't have to commute. Instead of spending an hour or more on a bus or train, they can start working as soon as they wake up. This also means they save money on transport, fuel and sometimes even work clothes. Second, many people find that they are more productive at home because there are fewer distractions. In an office, colleagues often interrupt you to chat or ask questions, but at home, you can focus on your work more easily. Third, working from home gives people more flexibility. They can take a break whenever they want, do some housework during the day, or spend more time with their children.

However, working from home also has some disadvantages. One of the biggest problems is loneliness. When you work alone at home every day, you can start to feel isolated. You miss the social interaction with your colleagues — the conversations at the coffee machine, the jokes during lunch, the feeling of being part of a team. Some people also find it hard to separate their work life from their personal life. When your office is in your home, it's difficult to "switch off" at the end of the day. Many remote workers say they work longer hours than they did in the office because they don't have a clear end to their working day.

Another challenge is that not everyone has a good space to work at home. Some people live in small flats and don't have a spare room for an office. They have to work at the kitchen table or on the sofa, which is not comfortable or good for their health. Also, people who live with children or other family members can find it hard to concentrate because of the noise and interruptions.

Today, many companies offer a "hybrid" model, which means employees work some days at home and some days in the office. This is often seen as the best solution because it gives workers the benefits of both options. They can enjoy the flexibility and quiet of working from home, but they can also go to the office to meet their colleagues, attend face-to-face meetings, and feel part of a team.

A recent survey showed that 73% of workers prefer the hybrid model. Only 12% want to go back to the office full-time, and 15% prefer to work from home every day. It seems clear that the way we work has changed forever, and both companies and workers are still learning how to make it work best for everyone.

**Glossary / Glossário:**

**Comprehension Questions / Perguntas de Interpretação:**

1. What was the daily routine of most office workers before 2020?
2. Name two advantages of working from home mentioned in the text.
3. Why do some remote workers feel lonely?
4. What is the "hybrid" model of work?
5. According to the survey, what percentage of workers prefer the hybrid model?

communication.title: Dialogue 1: At the Doctor's (No Médico)
communication.content:
**Characters:** Dr. Patel (Doctor), Tom (Patient), Receptionist

*(Tom enters the clinic and walks to the reception desk)*

**Receptionist:** Good morning. How can I help you?

**Tom:** Good morning. I have an appointment with Dr. Patel at 10 o'clock. My name is Tom Wilson.

**Receptionist:** Let me check... Yes, here you are. Tom Wilson, 10 a.m. with Dr. Patel. Please take a seat in the waiting room. The doctor will call you soon.

**Tom:** Thank you.

*(Tom sits in the waiting room. After about ten minutes, Dr. Patel opens his door.)*

**Dr. Patel:** Tom Wilson?

**Tom:** Yes, that's me.

**Dr. Patel:** Come in, please. Have a seat. So, what can I do for you today?

**Tom:** Well, doctor, I've been feeling quite unwell for the last few days. I have a terrible headache that won't go away, and my throat is very sore. I also have a cough, especially at night, and I feel very tired all the time.

**Dr. Patel:** I see. How long have you had these symptoms?

**Tom:** The sore throat started about four days ago. The headache and the cough started two days after that. And the tiredness... well, I've been tired for about a week now.

**Dr. Patel:** Do you have a fever?

**Tom:** I'm not sure. I feel hot and cold at the same time, if that makes sense. I haven't taken my temperature.

**Dr. Patel:** Let me check. *(Takes Tom's temperature.)* Yes, you have a slight fever — 37.8 degrees. Let me also look at your throat... Open your mouth and say "aah."

**Tom:** Aah.

**Dr. Patel:** Yes, your throat is quite red and swollen. I'm going to listen to your chest now. Take a deep breath, please... and again... Good. Your lungs sound clear, which is good news.

**Tom:** So what do you think it is, doctor?

**Dr. Patel:** It looks like you have a common cold with a mild throat infection. It's nothing serious, but you need to rest. I'm going to give you a prescription for some medicine. Take these tablets three times a day with food, and also take some paracetamol for the headache and fever. Drink plenty of water and hot drinks, like tea with honey, and try to rest as much as possible.

**Tom:** How long will it take to get better?

**Dr. Patel:** You should start to feel better in three or four days. If you don't feel better after a week, or if you get worse — for example, if your fever goes above 39 degrees, or if you have difficulty breathing — come back and see me immediately.

**Tom:** OK. Should I stay at home from work?

**Dr. Patel:** Yes, I'd recommend staying at home for at least two or three days. You need to rest, and you don't want to spread the infection to your colleagues.

**Tom:** All right. Thank you, doctor.

**Dr. Patel:** You're welcome. Take care, Tom. I hope you feel better soon.

**Tom:** Thank you. Goodbye.

*(Tom goes back to the reception desk.)*

**Receptionist:** Everything OK?

**Tom:** Yes, thank you. The doctor gave me a prescription. Where is the nearest pharmacy?

**Receptionist:** There's one just around the corner, on High Street. Turn left when you go out of the building and it's about 50 metres on the right.

**Tom:** Great. Thank you very much.

**Receptionist:** You're welcome. Get well soon!

---

communication.title: Dialogue 2: Checking in at a Hotel (Fazendo Check-in no Hotel)
communication.content:
**Characters:** Receptionist (R), Guest — Maria (M)

**R:** Good evening. Welcome to the Grand Park Hotel. How can I help you?

**M:** Good evening. I have a reservation under the name Maria Santos.

**R:** Let me look that up for you... Maria Santos... Yes, I can see your booking. A double room for three nights, checking in today, the 15th of March, and checking out on the 18th. Is that correct?

**M:** Yes, that's right.

**R:** Perfect. Could I see your passport or ID, please?

**M:** Of course. Here you are.

**R:** Thank you. *(Types on the computer.)* And could you fill in this registration form, please? I just need your full name, address and signature at the bottom.

**M:** Sure. *(Fills in the form.)* Here you go.

**R:** Thank you very much. Now, your room is number 412, on the fourth floor. Here is your key card. The lifts are just over there on your right.

**M:** Thank you. Is breakfast included?

**R:** Yes, breakfast is included in the price. It is served in the restaurant on the ground floor, from 7 a.m. to 10 a.m.

**M:** Great. And what time is check-out?

**R:** Check-out is at 11 a.m. However, if you need a late check-out, please let us know in advance and we can arrange it for you, depending on availability. There may be an extra charge.

**M:** OK, thank you. I have another question. Is there Wi-Fi in the hotel?

**R:** Yes, there is free Wi-Fi throughout the hotel. The network name is "GrandParkGuest" and the password is on this card, next to the room number.

**M:** Excellent. One more thing — I need to get to the city centre tomorrow morning. What is the best way to get there?

**R:** The city centre is about 15 minutes by bus. There's a bus stop right outside the hotel. Bus number 23 goes directly to the main square. Buses run every 10 minutes during the day. You can also take a taxi — it takes about 10 minutes and costs around 8 to 10 euros.

**M:** That's very helpful. Thank you so much.

**R:** You're welcome, Ms Santos. If you need anything at all during your stay, please don't hesitate to call reception. Our number is 0 on the phone in your room. Enjoy your stay!

**M:** Thank you very much. Good evening.

**R:** Good evening.

---

communication.title: Dialogue 3: At the Restaurant (No Restaurante)
communication.content:
**Characters:** Waiter (W), Customer 1 — James (J), Customer 2 — Lisa (L)

**W:** Good evening! Welcome to The Green Garden. Do you have a reservation?

**J:** Yes, a table for two under the name James Cooper.

**W:** Let me check... James Cooper, yes, table for two at 7:30. Right this way, please. *(Leads them to the table.)* Here you are. Can I get you something to drink while you look at the menu?

**J:** Yes, please. I'll have a glass of red wine.

**L:** And I'll have sparkling water, please.

**W:** Of course. I'll be right back.

*(The waiter brings the drinks.)*

**W:** Here you are. Red wine and sparkling water. Are you ready to order, or do you need a few more minutes?

**L:** I think we're ready. Can I ask a question first? What is the soup of the day?

**W:** Today's soup is tomato and basil.

**L:** That sounds lovely. I'll have the soup as a starter. And for the main course, I'd like the grilled salmon with roasted vegetables, please.

**W:** Excellent choice. And for you, sir?

**J:** For the starter, I'll have the mushroom bruschetta. And for the main, I'd like the beef steak, please. How is the steak cooked?

**W:** How would you like it? We can do it rare, medium-rare, medium or well-done.

**J:** Medium, please. And does it come with chips?

**W:** Yes, it comes with chips and a green salad.

**J:** Perfect.

**W:** Great. So that's tomato soup and grilled salmon for the lady, and mushroom bruschetta and medium steak for the gentleman. I'll bring your starters shortly.

*(The waiter brings the starters.)*

**W:** Here we are. Tomato and basil soup, and mushroom bruschetta. Enjoy your meal.

**J:** Thank you.

*(They eat their starters. The waiter comes back.)*

**W:** How was everything?

**L:** The soup was delicious, thank you.

**J:** The bruschetta was very good too.

**W:** Wonderful. I'll bring your main courses now.

*(The waiter brings the main courses.)*

**W:** Grilled salmon with roasted vegetables, and medium steak with chips and salad. Can I get you anything else? More drinks, perhaps?

**J:** Yes, I'd like another glass of wine, please.

**L:** I'm fine, thank you.

**W:** Of course. I'll bring that right away.

*(They finish their main courses. The waiter returns.)*

**W:** Would you like to see the dessert menu?

**L:** Oh, I'm very full, but... why not? What do you have?

**W:** We have chocolate brownie with vanilla ice cream, apple crumble with custard, lemon cheesecake, and a fresh fruit salad.

**L:** I'll have the lemon cheesecake, please.

**J:** And I'll have the chocolate brownie with ice cream.

**W:** Excellent. Would you also like coffee or tea?

**J:** I'll have a black coffee after dessert, please.

**L:** And I'll have a peppermint tea.

**W:** Perfect.

*(After dessert and hot drinks.)*

**J:** Excuse me, could we have the bill, please?

**W:** Of course. Here you are. *(Hands them the bill.)*

**J:** Can I pay by card?

**W:** Yes, of course. We accept all major cards. *(Brings the card machine.)* Just tap or insert your card... That's gone through. Thank you very much.

**J:** Thank you. The food was excellent.

**W:** Thank you very much. I'm glad you enjoyed it. Have a lovely evening.

**L:** Thank you. Goodnight!

**W:** Goodnight!

---

communication.title: Dialogue 4: At the Supermarket / Asking for Directions (No Supermercado / Pedindo Informações)
communication.content:
**Characters:** Customer — Ana (A), Shop Assistant (S), Stranger on the street (St)

*(Ana is in the supermarket, looking for something.)*

**A:** Excuse me, could you help me? I'm looking for olive oil. I can't find it anywhere.

**S:** Of course. Olive oil is in aisle 5, next to the vinegar and the other cooking oils. If you go straight down here and turn left at the end, you'll see it on your right.

**A:** Thank you very much. Oh, and do you sell fresh bread here?

**S:** Yes, the bakery section is at the back of the store, near the deli counter. You can get fresh bread, rolls and croissants there.

**A:** Perfect. One more thing — do you have any mangoes? I looked in the fruit section but I couldn't find any.

**S:** I'm afraid we don't have any mangoes at the moment. We're expecting a delivery tomorrow morning. They should be available from about 10 a.m.

**A:** Oh, that's a shame. Never mind. Thank you for your help.

**S:** You're welcome. If you need anything else, just ask.

*(Ana finishes her shopping and goes to the checkout.)*

**Cashier:** Hello. Would you like a bag?

**A:** Yes, please. Just one.

**Cashier:** That'll be £27.45, please.

**A:** Can I pay by card?

**Cashier:** Yes, of course. Just tap your card on the machine... Done. Here's your receipt.

**A:** Thank you. Have a nice day.

**Cashier:** You too!

*(Ana leaves the supermarket. She is looking for a post office and asks a stranger on the street.)*

**A:** Excuse me, sorry to bother you. Could you tell me where the nearest post office is?

**St:** The post office? Yes, of course. It's not far from here. Go straight along this road for about 200 metres. Then you'll see a large church on your left. Turn right at the church, into Park Lane. The post office is about 50 metres down Park Lane, on the left side, between a shoe shop and a coffee shop. You can't miss it.

**A:** So, straight ahead, right at the church, and then on the left?

**St:** That's right. It should take you about five minutes on foot.

**A:** Thank you so much. That's very kind of you.

**St:** No problem. Have a good day!

**A:** You too!

---

communication.title: Dialogue 5: Making Plans with a Friend (Fazendo Planos com um Amigo)
communication.content:
**Characters:** Carlos (C), David (D) — speaking on the phone

**C:** Hello?

**D:** Hi Carlos, it's David. How are you?

**C:** Oh, hey David! I'm good, thanks. A bit tired — I had a really long day at work today. How about you?

**D:** I'm fine, thanks. Listen, are you free this Saturday? I was thinking we could do something together.

**C:** This Saturday? Let me think... Yes, I'm free. I don't have any plans. What did you have in mind?

**D:** Well, the weather is supposed to be nice, so I thought we could go for a bike ride in the morning and then have lunch somewhere nice. There's a new Italian restaurant on Bridge Street that I've been wanting to try.

**C:** That sounds great! I love Italian food. Where shall we go for the bike ride?

**D:** How about the river path? It's really nice — about 15 kilometres long, flat and easy. It goes along the river and through the park. We could start at the car park near the bridge and ride to the end and back.

**C:** Perfect. I haven't been cycling for a while, so an easy ride sounds ideal. What time shall we meet?

**D:** How about 9 o'clock at the car park? That way, we can ride for a couple of hours and still be done in time for lunch.

**C:** 9 o'clock works for me. And what time should we book the restaurant for?

**D:** I'll book it for 12:30. That gives us enough time to finish the ride and get changed.

**C:** Good idea. Do I need to bring anything?

**D:** Just your bike and a bottle of water. Oh, and maybe a jacket, in case it gets cold. You know how the weather can change quickly here.

**C:** Sure. I'll also bring some cash for lunch, or do they take cards?

**D:** I think they take cards, but I'll check when I make the reservation. I'll send you a message later to confirm everything.

**C:** Great. I'm really looking forward to it. It's been ages since we hung out together.

**D:** I know! We've both been so busy with work. It'll be nice to relax and catch up.

**C:** Definitely. OK, so I'll see you on Saturday at 9 at the car park.

**D:** See you then. Have a good evening, Carlos.

**C:** You too, David. Thanks for calling. Bye!

**D:** Bye!

---
